turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Hey B, I was reading this article about kids these days using AI for everything, even homework. Don't you think it's messing up their brains? Back in our day, we had to memorize stuff.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Yeah, A, totally. My nephew's in high school, uses ChatGPT for essays. Teachers can't even tell anymore. What happened to good old books?

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Exactly. My grandma always said, 'The ancestors didn't have computers, and they built pyramids. What's our excuse?' Tradition worked for thousands of years.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Haha, pyramids, nice. But uh, isn't tech making life easier? Like, medicine and stuff?

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Sure, medicine's good, but... my dad told me, elders in the village said knowledge comes from the soil and stories passed down. Not from some machine spitting out answers. Remember how we learned farming from grandpa?

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Yeah, those long afternoons in the field. He knew every plant by heart. But come on, A, with apps now, farmers check weather instantly. Isn't that better?

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Maybe short-term, but ancestors warned against shortcuts. 'Rely on your hands and memory, or you'll forget your roots,' my uncle quotes from the old proverbs all the time. Look at us—do we even know how to plant without GPS?

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Hmm, fair point. I tried gardening last summer, total fail without YouTube. But traditions can be slow, right? Like, arranged marriages—my family pushed that, but love marriages seem happier now.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Whoa, tangent there. But no, elders say, 'The river flows as the ancestors dug it.' Arranged ones built strong families for generations. My cousins with love matches are divorcing left and right. Coincidence?

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Oof, yeah, my one cousin too. Still, science says compatibility tests and stuff work better. Not just old ways.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Science schmi-ence. Our great-grandpa lived to 95 on herbal remedies from ancestral recipes. No pills. Tradition's got the real wisdom tested by time.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Touché. I guess I'm half convinced. But what about women working? Ancestors kept them home, but now they're CEOs.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Um, hesitation here... yeah, grandma said, 'A woman's strength is in the hearth, holding the family like the earth holds roots.' But okay, some balance maybe. Still, divorce rates sky-high since everyone works.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Stats check out, I think. Alright, A, you're winning me over. Maybe we should teach kids those old stories more. Pass on the wisdom.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: That's the spirit! As the proverb goes, 'Ignore the ancestors, and the future crumbles.' Let's grab tea and swap more stories sometime.

input.objective: Debating modern technology and changes against ancestral traditions and wisdom
